content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      : File                                    Date  : Feb. 3, 1977

From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Ken Finney

Subject: Telephone conversation with Judge August Goebel.


Judge Goebel of the Los Angeles County Superior Court
called and asked a series of questions about his Statement
of Economic Interests.

First, he inquired about some property that his wife owns
in Arizona and from which she derives income in the form
of rent. In addition, last year she received income by reason of the sale of a portion of the land. Finally, she also
receives director's fees in exchange for sitting on the board
of a trust which is presently administering the land. The
Judge indicated that all of the income which she gets in connection with this land is her separate property. He pointed
out that she received the property as an inheritance and that
they have been careful to maintain the property in her separate
rate property. I informed the Judge that he would not incur
any reporting obligation by reason of the income his wife derives from the property because his reporting obligations with
respect to his wife's income would ensue only if he has a community property interest in the income. Moreover, I informed
the Judge that he would not have to disclose the property as
an interest in real property because it is located outside the
jurisdiction.

The Judge also inquired about how to evaluate the income received when he sold his house. He wondered whether he only had
to report the capital gain and, if so, how to measure it. I
informed him that he should disclose based on the total price
he received when he sold the house because income includes both
the capital gain and the return of capital which he received in
connection with the sale.

Finally, the Judge inquired about reporting a loan which he obtained to purchase a new home. I informed him that the loan
should be disclosed on the income schedule.

File
Feb. 3, 1977
P.2

The Judge was concerned about two other matters. First,
he thought that the verification which stated that he had
disclosed all investments and interests in real property was
inappropriate for him because he was not disclosing his wife's
interest in real property in Arizona. I indicated to him that
the definition of interests in real property is limited to those
in the jurisdiction and hence he could sign the verification.
However, I also told him that if he preferred to alter the
verification to say that he is reporting all interests in real
property which are reportable under the Act, that would be
permissible. I also told the Judge that with respect to any of
the reporting of income in connection with the loan for the
purchase of a new home and the sale of the old home he could
append notations to explain how this income came about. He
was concerned that it would be misleading to just indicate that
he had received these amounts of income. I responded that he
is always free to attach a notation to explain anything which
he feels might create a misconception.


KF:jo
